method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the South Pacific Ocean, in the western Solomon Islands about 24 km south of Vangunu Island.[2]  
It lies in a convergent tectonic setting on the Pacific Ring of Fire, where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[1][2]  
The volcanic edifice rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, and its summit lies close to the sea surface, with general descriptions placing it about 20 m below sea level and bathymetric observations recording the crater rim at an average depth of about 24 m.[2][5]  
Bathymetric surveys describe the main summit as an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m by 75 m, with the deepest soundings on the peak at about 70 m below sea level.[5]  
The volcano’s flanks descend at slopes of about 18° to depths greater than 1,000 m, giving Kavachi a prominent seamount form above the surrounding seafloor.[5]  
A secondary summit, described as a southwest extension, lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and rises to a depth of about 260 m below sea level.[5]  
Kavachi is known for frequent phreatomagmatic and subaerial eruptions, and its shallow summit has repeatedly produced short-lived islands that are later removed by wave action and erosion.[3][4][5]  
Because the summit is near the ocean surface, eruptions can produce discoloured water plumes visible from aircraft and satellites, as well as ash, steam, and lava ejections above sea level during more vigorous activity.[2][4]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi has been one of the most frequently active volcanoes in the Pacific, with its first historically recorded eruption in 1939 and near-surface eruptive episodes recurring every few years.[1] Since 1939, eruptions have produced temporary islands at least nine times, but these features were generally too small and insufficiently protected by solid lava to withstand wave erosion, disappearing within months.[1] In late 1951, activity at the volcano was associated with frequent small tsunamis, with one account giving a possible date of 22 December 1951.[1]

On 14 May 2000, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel *Franklin* observed a renewed island-building phase in which ash and incandescent lava blocks were ejected up to 70 m above sea level, while sulfurous steam plumes rose to about 500 m.[2] That episode followed about nine years of dormancy and included the development of a sandy, ashy summit beach about 2 m below sea level.[2] Explosive eruptions occurred daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and on 13 January 2002 volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the volcano, accompanied by frequent loud noises.[2] During an eruptive cycle in November 2003, a 15 m-high island formed, but by the 16th the summit had been reduced to about 32 m below sea level and no evidence of volcanic activity was observed for the first time in four years.[2] Renewed activity was reported on 15 March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2]

A January 2015 expedition coincided with an unusual lull in activity, allowing researchers to examine the crater and flanks directly despite Kavachi’s normally explosive behavior.[3] Work from that expedition described warm water, carbon dioxide degassing, and hydrothermal plume activity, indicating that the volcano remained hydrothermally active during the lull.[3][4] Petrological observations from samples collected at the summit suggested that Kavachi’s basaltic to mafic eruptive activity is driven in part by volatile degassing.[4]

A later summary based on Global Volcanism Program data counted 33 historical eruptions, with activity recorded across 44 of the 87 years since 1939, although the remoteness of the volcano means earlier events may have been missed.[1] As of May 2026, the most recent listed eruptive episode had begun in 2021 and was still continuing.[1]

==Marine life==
During quiescent periods, surveys have recorded a biological community within Kavachi’s crater despite acidic, mineral-rich hydrothermal conditions.[2][4] A 2015 expedition reported gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, and two shark species: the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*).[4][5] The sharks were filmed by baited cameras swimming inside the crater and reacting aggressively to bait, helping popularize Kavachi’s nickname as “Sharkcano” or “Sharkano”.[4][5] Measurements above the crater indicated water temperatures up to 13 °C warmer than surrounding seawater, reaching about 42 °C, with pH estimated at around 6.1 and elevated volcanic gases including carbon dioxide and sulfur compounds.[4] Other robotic observations have similarly reported abrupt pH decreases, elevated temperatures, and volcanic gases in the seawater.[1]

The presence of large predators in such conditions has been interpreted cautiously, as it remains unclear whether sharks reside in the crater continuously or move in and out according to changes in volcanic activity.[1][5] Suggested explanations include behavioral avoidance of eruptive phases, sensitivity to chemical or seismic warning cues, and physiological tolerance of warm, acidic water.[1] The hydrothermal plume may also support adapted microbial populations, including sulfur-oxidizing bacteria such as *Sulfurimonas*, which can use sulfur compounds and carbon dioxide in chemosynthesis.[4] Because it combines hydrothermal activity with sharks, fish, zooplankton and microbial communities, Kavachi has been described as a natural laboratory for studying extreme marine ecosystems, biological adaptation to heat and acidity, and the effects of changing ocean chemistry.[1][5]

It lies in a convergent tectonic setting on the Pacific Ring of Fire, where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[1][2]
